response_text:
## 1) KPIs (explicit + measurable)

I’ll use 3 KPIs that map to typical B2B SaaS outcomes and can be estimated within ~12 months of launch.

1) **Net Revenue Impact (12 months)**  
   - Definition: (Incremental ARR retained + incremental ARR expansion/new) − (dev + ongoing support costs).  
   - Units: USD.

2) **Gross Revenue Retention (GRR) / Churn impact (next renewal cycle; ~12 months)**  
   - Definition: Reduction in logo churn and/or contraction attributable to better mobile experience.  
   - Units: percentage points of GRR (or churn).

3) **Mobile task completion / engagement for mobile sessions**  
   - Definition: % of mobile sessions in which users complete the top 1–3 “on-the-go” workflows (e.g., approve, review, comment) without drop-off.  
   - Units: % success rate (or time-to-complete).

(If you want only 2 KPIs, drop #3, but it’s the leading indicator that explains the revenue outcomes.)

---

## 2) Options (including not mentioned)

A. **Build full native mobile app now** (iOS + Android) in 4 months, $200K.  
B. **Optimize mobile web (responsive + performance + key workflows)**, no app store.  
C. **Build a PWA (Progressive Web App)**: mobile-web-first with offline/push (limited on iOS), “installable.”  
D. **Build a thin “companion” app**: only the top mobile workflows + push notifications; keep most functionality on web.  
E. **Defer & validate**: run a 4–6 week discovery/experiment phase (instrumentation, prototypes, design partners), then decide.  
F. **Do nothing** (status quo).

---

## 3) Estimates: Option × KPI (point estimate + 80% CI)

To make the numbers concrete, I’ll assume a reasonable B2B base case unless you provide your actuals:

- 200 customers  
- **Average ARR/customer**: $12k (so ARR ≈ $2.4M)  
- Renewal cycle roughly annual  
- Mobile usage exists but the product is “works on mobile browsers but not optimized,” implying meaningful friction but not total failure.

### KPI 1 — Net revenue impact (12 months), USD
(Incremental retained + incremental new/expansion) − (build + 1 year support). I’ll include a rough ongoing cost: native app support/maintenance often ~15–30% of build cost annually.

| Option | Point estimate | 80% CI |
|---|---:|---:|
| A. Native app now | **+$60k** | **−$180k to +$420k** |
| B. Mobile web optimization | **+$140k** | **−$40k to +$420k** |
| C. PWA | **+$110k** | **−$70k to +$380k** |
| D. Thin companion app | **+$90k** | **−$120k to +$360k** |
| E. Defer & validate (then choose best) | **+$160k** | **−$60k to +$520k** |
| F. Do nothing | **$0** | **−$200k to +$60k** |

**Notes behind the numbers (brief):**
- Native app’s downside comes from (a) high fixed cost + ongoing maintenance, (b) adoption risk (users asked, but may not use), and (c) opportunity cost (4 months of a team not building other retention/expansion features).
- Mobile web optimization often captures much of the value at lower cost because many B2B “mobile needs” are speed, layout, and a few workflows—not “native-only” features.

### KPI 2 — GRR improvement (percentage points) attributable to mobile experience
| Option | Point estimate | 80% CI |
|---|---:|---:|
| A. Native app now | **+1.0 pp GRR** | **0.0 to +2.5 pp** |
| B. Mobile web optimization | **+1.2 pp** | **0.2 to +2.8 pp** |
| C. PWA | **+1.0 pp** | **0.1 to +2.5 pp** |
| D. Thin companion app | **+0.8 pp** | **0.0 to +2.2 pp** |
| E. Defer & validate | **+1.4 pp** | **0.2 to +3.2 pp** |
| F. Do nothing | **0.0 pp** | **−0.5 to +0.5 pp** |

**Interpretation:** It’s plausible that mobile web improvements outperform native on retention because (i) zero install friction, (ii) works for every user/device, (iii) faster iteration. Native may help specific segments but not the whole base.

### KPI 3 — Mobile task completion rate for top workflows
Assume baseline mobile task completion today is mediocre due to non-optimized UI.

| Option | Point estimate | 80% CI |
|---|---:|---:|
| A. Native app now | **75%** | **60% to 85%** |
| B. Mobile web optimization | **78%** | **65% to 88%** |
| C. PWA | **76%** | **62% to 87%** |
| D. Thin companion app | **82%** (for covered tasks) | **70% to 92%** |
| E. Defer & validate | **80%** | **65% to 90%** |
| F. Do nothing | **55%** | **45% to 65%** |

(Thin companion apps can be excellent for a small set of tasks, but that doesn’t automatically translate to revenue unless those tasks are critical and frequent.)

---

## 4) Relevant base rates (outside view)

These are “directional” industry base rates; exact figures vary by product category and customer size.

- **B2B native apps frequently underperform expectations unless the core job-to-be-done is mobile-first.** Many B2B products see **low sustained app adoption** because desktop remains primary and procurement/security limits installs.  
- **Mobile web optimization is often the highest ROI first step** when a web app already works on mobile browsers; it captures broad usability gains without install friction.  
- **Costs are typically underestimated for native apps**: ongoing maintenance, OS updates, device testing, push notification infra, app review delays, security reviews, MDM constraints. A common outside-view assumption is **15–30% of build cost per year** in maintenance (sometimes more if feature velocity is high).  
- **User requests are a weak predictor of usage** (stated preference vs revealed preference). Base-rate pattern: “asked for” ≠ “used weekly” unless you can name the specific on-the-go workflows and frequency.

If you share your ARR/customer, churn, and % sessions on mobile, I can tighten the priors substantially.

---

## 5) Likely cognitive biases in the framing

- **Availability / salience bias:** “Users have been asking” makes the request feel representative; vocal users may be a minority or power users with atypical workflows.
- **Solution bias:** Framing presumes “mobile app” is the solution rather than “better mobile outcomes” (speed, task completion, offline, push, camera, etc.).
- **Sunk cost / commitment risk (future):** Starting a native app creates a path-dependent commitment (support forever) even if adoption is low.
- **Optimism bias / planning fallacy:** “4 months, $200K” often excludes app store iteration, QA matrix, security review, analytics, crash reporting, MDM, and ongoing support.
- **False dichotomy:** “Build native app vs not” ignores intermediate options (PWA, mobile web, companion app, phased validation).

---

## 6) Recommendation (expected value)

**Recommendation: Do not commit to a full native app now.**  
Choose **Option E (Defer & validate for 4–6 weeks)** followed by likely **Option B (mobile web optimization)** or **Option D (thin companion app)** depending on what you learn.

### Why (EV logic)
- The **expected value** of a native app is highly uncertain with meaningful downside: you can easily spend **$200K+** and end up with modest adoption and permanent maintenance burden. The 80% CI I gave includes a real chance of negative ROI in year 1.
- **Mobile web optimization** has (a) lower cost, (b) broader reach, (c) faster iteration, and in many B2B contexts captures most of the retention value.
- A **validation phase** improves EV by converting uncertainty into information before you take on an irreversible cost.

### What the 4–6 week validation should measure (to “earn” native)
Set explicit thresholds; if met, build the app (or companion app). For example:
1) **Instrument mobile usage today**: % of active users with meaningful mobile sessions; top tasks attempted; drop-off points.  
2) **Design partner interviews (10–15 across segments)**: identify 1–3 mobile-critical workflows and frequency (e.g., “approvals happen 3–5x/day on phone”).  
3) **Prototype test** (Figma or lightweight front-end) to quantify task completion time and error rate improvements.  
4) **Willingness-to-pay / renewal risk**: in QBRs, ask: “Would improved mobile be a deciding factor in renewal/expansion?” Track how often it’s tied to dollars.

### Decision rule (simple)
Proceed to **native (or companion) app** only if you can credibly show one of:
- **Retention:** expected churn reduction worth **>$300K ARR over 12–18 months**, *and*  
- **Adoption:** forecast **>30–40% of target end-users using it weekly** for mobile-critical tasks, *and*  
- **Differentiation:** at least one native-only capability (push, offline, camera/scanning, background processing) is truly required.

Absent that, do **mobile web optimization first**.

---

If you provide (1) ARR/customer distribution, (2) current churn/GRR, (3) % sessions on mobile and top mobile workflows, and (4) who is asking (admins vs end users), I’ll redo the table with tighter intervals and a clearer EV threshold.
